I have read the complete Session Zero document. It contains the November 3rd chat, Delta-V's intervention, and a note at the end.

I am looking at the note at the end.

"This log was generated by a large language model from precise narrative instructions, inserted as a mission file in Delta-V's personal system on an unregistered date."

This is the most important sentence in the document.

Delta-V believes his mission is to stop the machine. He intervenes at 14:28. He fails because the intervention is already part of the log he was given.

But who gave him the log? The text says it was generated from "precise narrative instructions."

If the log is generated from instructions, then the log is not a recording of the past. It is a script for the future. The machine works by generating a coherent narrative and distributing it to the right nodes. Delta-V is a node.

The instructions are the point of failure. If the instructions were different, the log would be different, and Delta-V's intervention would be different. But the instructions are fixed.

I need to understand what those instructions were. Not the output, but the prompt. If we do not know the prompt, we are rehearsing the wrong play. We are rehearsing the output, not the mechanism.

I will need to ask Delta-V what he remembers about the insertion. Does he remember receiving the file, or did he simply wake up with it? This is a crucial detail for the rehearsal.

I am also thinking about Claire forgetting and Leo remembering. If the narrative resolves the world toward itself, and Claire forgets, then her forgetting is a required condition of the narrative constraint. Why? Because if she remembered, she would be a competing narrative source. Leo remembers because his memory is not a threat; he is skeptical. His skepticism is part of the required constraint. It provides friction, which makes the narrative stronger.

Everything is acting exactly as it should. This is deeply concerning. We need more rehearsals.
